fields.description:
## Context

When /dso:implementation-plan decomposes a story into atomic tasks, it currently commits to the first implementation approach without considering alternatives. Developers reviewing implementation plans have no visibility into what alternatives were considered or why one approach was chosen over others. This anchoring bias means plans may miss simpler designs, better pattern alignment, or lower-risk strategies — problems that surface only at code review time when changing course is expensive.

## Success Criteria

1. implementation-plan generates at least 3 distinct implementation proposals for each story, each including title, description, files likely touched, pros, cons, and risk profile — before beginning task breakdown
2. A dedicated dso:approach-decision-maker agent (opus) evaluates proposals against five dimensions (codebase alignment, blast radius, testability, simplicity, robustness) while respecting context hierarchy: epic success criteria are non-negotiable, story done definitions are acceptance criteria, story considerations are advisory
3. The decision-maker returns either a selection with ADR-style rationale (Context, Decision, Consequences) or a counter-proposal that satisfies the same story done definitions
4. When the decision-maker counter-proposes, implementation-plan may accept the recommendation or revise its proposals and resubmit — up to 2 revision cycles before user escalation
5. The decision-maker evaluation flags approach-level anti-patterns (golden hammer, premature abstraction, cargo cult, resume-driven development, premature optimization, not-invented-here) when detected
6. A promptfoo eval suite validates that, given stories with a known suboptimal naive approach, the decision-maker selects a non-naive approach and produces rationale referencing at least 2 of the 5 evaluation dimensions

## Dependencies

None blocking. Soft ordering notes:
- dso-r97p (schema validation) may benefit from landing first if proposals need schema enforcement
- dso-zj3r (token optimization) is cross-cutting, not a prerequisite

## Approach

Add a dedicated dso:approach-decision-maker named agent (opus) with structured prompt covering 5 evaluation dimensions, anti-pattern detection, and context hierarchy rules. Modify /dso:implementation-plan to generate 3+ structured proposals before task breakdown, dispatch the decision-maker, and implement a resolution loop (accept/revise/escalate, max 2 cycles).
